items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.q: What is the denary value of binary 10110?
items.why: Students forget to apply place values. Each position is a power of 2 from right (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...). Reading binary as denary is the most common error.

items.q: What is the time complexity of binary search?
items.misconception: O(n) - because it searches through items.
items.why: Students conflate 'fast' with O(1). Binary search halves the search space each step, giving logarithmic time - much better than linear, but not constant.

items.q: Dijkstra's algorithm fails when edges have...
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Negative weights. “Equal weights” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does the 'h' in A*'s f(n) = g(n) + h(n) represent?
items.why: Correct answer: Heuristic estimate to goal. “History cost” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many queens are placed in the classic N-queens problem when N = 8?
items.why: The real figure is 8 queens. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
